summary: Gruber announces an experiment with Google's new AdSense program on Daring Fireball. He notes the irony that Google's contextual ads are pitching Mac OS X Jaguar when his latest article is about its successor, Panther. He reports earning roughly $0.10-0.30 per click but finds the ads aesthetically offensive because Google doesn't allow CSS customization. He frames it as a temporary experiment, inviting readers to click but not to game the system.
key_points: Google AdSense's contextual targeting is imprecise, serving ads for Jaguar on a site discussing its successor Panther
The inability to customize ad appearance via CSS is a dealbreaker for a design-conscious site
Per-click rates vary significantly ($0.10-$0.50) across different publishers with no transparency from Google
Gruber frames monetization as an experiment, prioritizing site aesthetics over revenue
money_quotes: If you haven't bought Jaguar already, somehow I doubt you're going to spend $120 on it now.
The downside, of course, is that the ads are hideously ugly.
I wouldn't hesitate to keep the ads if I were allowed to customize their display via CSS.
But as it stands, they'll have to pay me quite a bit of scratch to tempt me to keep the ads.
Remember, it's an experiment. Feel free to send me a dime by clicking an ad, but please don't game it by clicking madly.
It's humorous to see that Google thinks Hivelogic is a web site about cigars and wedding speeches.
key_insight: Early web advertising forced a tension between monetization and design standards, with Google's AdSense offering revenue at the cost of aesthetic control that design-minded publishers found unacceptable.
